title: Getting Started with birt
text: birt (Bayesian IRT) R package fitting Item Response Theory models using Bayesian estimation via CmdStan. supports three dichotomous IRT models: Rasch (1PL): Every item discrimination — items differ difficulty. 2PL: item gets discrimination parameter controlling sharply separates students different ability. 3PL: Adds guessing parameter representing probability correct response pure chance. models estimated using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via CmdStan, producing full posterior distributions every parameter. means get credible intervals, convergence diagnostics, posterior predictive checks — just point estimates.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Traditional IRT software uses Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). Bayesian estimation via MCMC offers several advantages: Full uncertainty quantification: get credible intervals every parameter, just point estimates. Works small samples: MLE can fail produce extreme estimates small samples. Priors stabilize Bayesian estimates. Missing data handled naturally: Bayesian framework simply skips unobserved responses. Model diagnostics built : Convergence diagnostics, trace plots, posterior predictive checks straightforward.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: birt requires CmdStan installed computer. Verify installation:
code: # Step 1: Install cmdstanr (the R interface to CmdStan) install.packages("cmdstanr", repos = c(   "https://stan-dev.r-universe.dev/",   getOption("repos") ))  # Step 2: Install CmdStan itself (~5 minutes) cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()  # Step 3: Install birt from GitHub install.packages("pak") pak::pak("Ndukaboika/birt") cmdstanr::cmdstan_version() library(birt)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: three models share core idea: probability correct response depends difference person’s ability item’s difficulty. differ many item parameters estimate.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: logit(P(Yjk=1))=αj+δ−βk\text{logit}(P(Y_{jk} = 1)) = \alpha_j + \delta - \beta_k : δ\delta = overall mean ability αj\alpha_j = student jj deviates mean βk\beta_k = difficulty item kk Total ability: θj=αj+δ\theta_j = \alpha_j + \delta ability equals difficulty (θj=βk\theta_j = \beta_k), student 50% chance answering correctly.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: logit(P(Yjk=1))=ak⋅(αj+δ−βk)\text{logit}(P(Y_{jk} = 1)) = a_k \cdot (\alpha_j + \delta - \beta_k) Adds discrimination aka_k. ak>1a_k > 1, item separates students sharply. ak<1a_k < 1, item less discriminating. ak=1a_k = 1, reduces Rasch model.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: P(Yjk=1)=ck+(1−ck)⋅logistic(ak⋅(αj+δ−βk))P(Y_{jk} = 1) = c_k + (1 - c_k) \cdot \text{logistic}(a_k \cdot (\alpha_j + \delta - \beta_k)) Adds guessing ckc_k. Even low-ability students probability ckc_k getting item right. 4-option multiple choice, expect ck≈0.25c_k \approx 0.25.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: birt uses weakly informative default priors work across wide range testing scenarios. defaults deliberately neutral — make strong assumptions test students. Users domain knowledge can override prior informative values.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: defaults informed recommendations Stan User’s Guide (Stan Development Team, 2024), Luo Jiao (2018), edstan package (Furr, 2017). ability prior also serves identify scale model.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: delta ~ Normal(0, 1): Centered zero — assumption whether students average difficulty. data determines . alpha ~ Normal(0, 1.5): Wide enough accommodate strong weak students. deviation 3 logits shifts P(correct) 50% 95%. beta ~ Normal(0, 1.5): rationale. Covers typical difficulty range well-constructed test items. ~ LogNormal(0, 0.5): Always positive (negative discrimination contradicts IRT assumptions), centered 1.0 (Rasch case). c ~ Beta(2, 8): Weakly informative mean around 0.2. Wide enough accommodate different item formats without making strong assumptions.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Users prior knowledge test can override default:
code: # If you know students tend to perform above average fit <- rasch_fit(data,   prior_delta = c(0.75, 0.5),   seed = 123 )  # If your items have tightly clustered difficulties fit <- rasch_fit(data,   prior_beta_sd = 0.5,   seed = 123 )  # 4-option multiple choice (guessing around 0.25) fit3 <- threepl_fit(data,   prior_c = c(5, 15),    # Beta(5,15), mean = 0.25   seed = 123 )  # 5-option multiple choice (guessing around 0.20) fit3 <- threepl_fit(data,   prior_c = c(5, 20),    # Beta(5,20), mean = 0.20   seed = 123 )  # Free-response items (very little guessing) fit3 <- threepl_fit(data,   prior_c = c(1, 19),    # Beta(1,19), mean = 0.05   seed = 123 )  # Wide priors for large samples (let data fully dominate) fit <- rasch_fit(data,   prior_delta    = c(0, 3),   prior_alpha_sd = 3,   prior_beta_sd  = 3,   seed = 123 )

title: Getting Started with birt
text: priors stored fitted object:
code: fit <- rasch_fit(data, seed = 123) fit$priors

title: Getting Started with birt
text: rasch_simulate() generates data known true parameters can verify model recovers correct values.
code: library(birt)  sim <- rasch_simulate(   J = 300,            # 300 students   K = 10,             # 10 items   delta_true = 0.75,  # mean ability   alpha_sd = 1,       # spread of abilities   seed = 42           # reproducible )  # The response matrix head(sim$data)  # True values we want to recover sim$beta    # item difficulties sim$delta   # mean ability

title: Getting Started with birt
code: fit <- rasch_fit(   data = sim$data,   chains = 4,   parallel_chains = 4,   iter_sampling = 1000,   seed = 123 )  # Quick overview fit  # Detailed summary summary(fit)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: summary() output contains: Mean Ability (delta): estimated overall mean ability credible interval. Compare sim$delta check recovery. Item Difficulties (beta): One row per item posterior mean (point estimate), credible interval, convergence diagnostics. Check Rhat < 1.01 ESS > 400 items. Person Ability Summary: Overview estimated abilities across students.

title: Getting Started with birt
code: # Item difficulties item_params(fit)  # Person abilities (total: alpha + delta) head(person_params(fit), 10)  # Mean ability delta_param(fit)  # Change credible interval width item_params(fit, prob = 0.90)  # 90% interval item_params(fit, prob = 0.99)  # 99% interval

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Outfit infit mean-square statistics measuring well item conforms model. near 1.0. Interpretation: Outfit > 1.3: item behaves erratically — unexpected responses students far item’s difficulty level. Outfit < 0.7: item predictable — possibly redundant items. Infit: interpretation sensitive systematic patterns near item’s difficulty less affected outliers.
code: ifit <- item_fit(fit) ifit  # Flag misfitting items ifit[ifit$outfit > 1.3 | ifit$outfit < 0.7, ]

title: Getting Started with birt
text: High person outfit may indicate guessing, careless responding, cheating.
code: pfit <- person_fit(fit) head(pfit)  # Unusual response patterns pfit[pfit$outfit > 1.3, ]

previous_headings: Quick Start with Simulated Data > Plots
title: Getting Started with birt
text: curve shows P(correct) function ability. Harder items shifted right. Shaded bands show 95% credible intervals.
code: plot(fit, type = "icc")  # Specific items only plot_icc(fit, items = c(1, 5, 10))  # Without credible bands plot_icc(fit, ci = FALSE)

previous_headings: Quick Start with Simulated Data > Plots
title: Getting Started with birt
text: Places students (histogram) items (triangles) logit scale. reveals whether test well-targeted: Items covering full student range = good targeting. items left students = test easy. Gaps items = poor measurement ability range.
code: plot(fit, type = "wright")

previous_headings: Quick Start with Simulated Data > Plots
title: Getting Started with birt
text: Shows ability scale test precise. Higher information means precise measurement.
code: # Total test information plot(fit, type = "info")  # With individual item curves plot_info(fit, show_items = TRUE)

previous_headings: Quick Start with Simulated Data > Plots
title: Getting Started with birt
text: Check MCMC convergence. Chains overlap look like “fuzzy caterpillars.” chains stuck different places trending, model hasn’t converged.
code: plot(fit, type = "trace")

title: Getting Started with birt
text: simulated data, verify model recovers truth:
code: items_est <- item_params(fit)  # Plot true vs estimated plot(sim$beta, items_est$mean,      xlab = "True Difficulty",      ylab = "Estimated Difficulty",      pch = 19, main = "Parameter Recovery") abline(0, 1, col = "red", lty = 2)  # Correlation (should be > 0.95) cor(sim$beta, items_est$mean)  # Delta recovery cat("True delta:", sim$delta, "\n") cat("Estimated delta:", delta_param(fit)$mean, "\n")

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Use 2PL suspect items differ well discriminate high- low-ability students.
code: fit2 <- twopl_fit(sim$data, seed = 123) summary(fit2)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Interpretation: ≈1a \approx 1: Similar Rasch — average discrimination. >1.5a > 1.5: Highly discriminating — steep ICC, strongly separates students. <0.5a < 0.5: Poorly discriminating — flat ICC. Consider removing item.
code: discrim_params(fit2)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Notice different slopes ICCs — steeper curves correspond higher discrimination:
code: plot(fit2, type = "icc") plot(fit2, type = "info")

title: Getting Started with birt
code: fit2_custom <- twopl_fit(sim$data,   prior_a = c(0, 1),         # wider discrimination prior   prior_delta = c(0.5, 0.5), # informative: students above average   seed = 123 ) fit2_custom$priors

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Use 3PL multiple-choice tests guessing plausible. Requires 500+ students stable estimation.
code: sim_large <- rasch_simulate(J = 500, K = 10, seed = 42)  fit3 <- threepl_fit(sim_large$data, seed = 123) summary(fit3)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Interpretation: c≈0.25c \approx 0.25: Typical 4-option multiple choice. c≈0c \approx 0: guessing (expected free-response items). c>0.35c > 0.35: Unusually high — item may poor distractors.
code: guessing_params(fit3)

title: Getting Started with birt
code: # 4-option multiple choice fit3_mc4 <- threepl_fit(sim_large$data,   prior_c = c(5, 15),   # Beta(5,15), mean = 0.25   seed = 123 )  # 5-option multiple choice fit3_mc5 <- threepl_fit(sim_large$data,   prior_c = c(5, 20),   # Beta(5,20), mean = 0.20   seed = 123 )  # Free-response items fit3_fr <- threepl_fit(sim_large$data,   prior_c = c(1, 19),   # Beta(1,19), mean = 0.05   seed = 123 )

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Notice lower asymptote — P(correct) never reaches zero:
code: plot(fit3, type = "icc")

title: Getting Started with birt
text: birt includes 1,382 students 12-item algebra test.
code: data(algebra) dim(algebra) colMeans(algebra, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with birt
code: fit_alg <- rasch_fit(algebra, seed = 123) summary(fit_alg)  # Hardest items items <- item_params(fit_alg) items[order(items$mean, decreasing = TRUE), ]  # Item fit item_fit(fit_alg)  # Plots plot(fit_alg, type = "icc") plot(fit_alg, type = "wright") plot(fit_alg, type = "info")

what: Compare Rasch vs 2PL on Real Data
title: Getting Started with birt
code: fit_alg_2pl <- twopl_fit(algebra, seed = 123)  # Are discriminations similar or very different? discrim_params(fit_alg_2pl)  # Compare ICCs plot(fit_alg, type = "icc")      # Rasch: parallel curves plot(fit_alg_2pl, type = "icc")  # 2PL: varying slopes

title: Getting Started with birt
text: data matrix data frame: Rows = students (examinees, respondents) Columns = items (questions, tasks) Values = 0 (incorrect) 1 (correct) NA = missing (handled automatically)
code: my_data <- read.csv("my_test_data.csv", row.names = 1) my_data <- as.matrix(my_data)  # Verify format dim(my_data) table(my_data, useNA = "ifany")  # Fit fit_mine <- rasch_fit(my_data, seed = 123) summary(fit_mine)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: sensitivity analysis checks whether results depend prior. Re-fit model different priors compare. estimates barely change, conclusions data-driven.
code: # Default weakly informative priors fit_default <- rasch_fit(algebra, seed = 123)  # Tighter priors fit_tight <- rasch_fit(algebra,   prior_alpha_sd = 0.5,   prior_beta_sd = 0.5,   seed = 123 )  # Very wide priors fit_wide <- rasch_fit(algebra,   prior_delta    = c(0, 5),   prior_alpha_sd = 3,   prior_beta_sd  = 3,   seed = 123 )  # Compare item difficulty estimates items_default <- item_params(fit_default) items_tight   <- item_params(fit_tight) items_wide    <- item_params(fit_wide)  # Correlations above 0.99 indicate results are robust to prior choice cor(items_default$mean, items_tight$mean) cor(items_default$mean, items_wide$mean)  # Visual comparison plot(items_default$mean, items_wide$mean,      xlab = "Default Priors", ylab = "Wide Priors",      pch = 19, main = "Prior Sensitivity") abline(0, 1, col = "red", lty = 2)

title: Getting Started with birt
code: # The CmdStanMCMC object class(fit$fit)  # Raw draws for custom analysis draws <- fit$fit$draws(variables = "beta", format = "draws_matrix") dim(draws)  # Save a fitted model fit$fit$save_object("my_fit.rds")

title: Getting Started with birt
code: # More iterations for better estimates fit <- rasch_fit(data, iter_sampling = 2000, seed = 123)  # Fix divergent transitions fit <- rasch_fit(data, adapt_delta = 0.95, seed = 123)  # Fewer chains for speed (not recommended for final analysis) fit <- rasch_fit(data, chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2, seed = 123)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Start Rasch. Simplest interpretable. item fit statistics acceptable (outfit/infit 0.7 1.3), stop . Try 2PL Rasch fit poor theoretical reasons expect varying discrimination. Compare ICCs — slopes differ meaningfully, discrimination parameter capturing real differences. Try 3PL multiple-choice items, 500+ students, clear evidence guessing. estimated guessing parameters near zero, 2PL sufficient.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: sampler trouble exploring posterior. Try increasing adapt_delta: Values 0.9 0.99 usually help.
code: fit <- rasch_fit(data, adapt_delta = 0.95, seed = 123)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: enough effective samples. Try iterations:
code: fit <- rasch_fit(data, iter_sampling = 2000, seed = 123)

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Stan model compiles C++ first use (~30-60 seconds). cached afterward, subsequent calls fast.

title: Getting Started with birt
text: see “lazy-load database corrupt”, reinstall :
code: remove.packages("birt") devtools::install(args = "--no-byte-compile")

title: Getting Started with birt
text: Luo, Y., & Jiao, H. (2018). Using Stan program Bayesian item response theory. Educational Psychological Measurement, 78(3), 384-408. Stan Development Team (2024). Stan User’s Guide, Section 1.11: Item-Response Theory Models. Gelman, ., Jakulin, ., Pittau, M. G., & Su, Y. S. (2008). weakly informative default prior distribution logistic regression models. Annals Applied Statistics, 2(4), 1360-1383. Culpepper, S. . (2016). Revisiting 4-parameter item response model: Bayesian estimation application. Psychometrika, 81(4), 1142-1163. Furr, D. C. (2017). edstan: Stan models item response theory. R package.

code: @Manual{,   title = {birt: Bayesian Rasch Models via CmdStan},   author = {Nduka Boika},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/ndukaboika/birt}, }

title: Bayesian Rasch Models via CmdStan
text: birt (Bayesian IRT) fits Item Response Theory models using Bayesian estimation via CmdStan. Stan models pre-compiled package installation using instantiate package, compilation delay runtime. Supported models: priors user-configurable. Sensible defaults provided based Stan User’s Guide.

title: Bayesian Rasch Models via CmdStan
code: # 1. Install cmdstanr install.packages("cmdstanr", repos = c(   "https://stan-dev.r-universe.dev/",   getOption("repos") ))  # 2. Install CmdStan cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()  # 3. Install birt (from source to trigger Stan compilation) pak::pak("Ndukaboika/birt")

title: Bayesian Rasch Models via CmdStan
code: library(birt)  # Simulate data sim <- rasch_simulate(J = 300, K = 10, seed = 42)  # Fit a Rasch model fit <- rasch_fit(sim$data, seed = 123) fit summary(fit)  # Extract parameters item_params(fit) delta_param(fit)  # Plots plot(fit, type = "icc") plot(fit, type = "wright") plot(fit, type = "info")  # Diagnostics item_fit(fit)

title: Bayesian Rasch Models via CmdStan
text: Every prior can overridden:
code: fit <- rasch_fit(data,   prior_delta    = c(0, 2),       # Normal(0, 2) for mean ability   prior_alpha_sd = 2,             # Normal(0, 2) for ability deviations   prior_beta_sd  = 2,             # Normal(0, 2) for item difficulties   seed = 123 )  # 3PL for 4-option multiple choice fit3 <- threepl_fit(data,   prior_c = c(5, 15),             # Beta(5, 15), mean = 0.25   seed = 123 )

title: Bayesian Rasch Models via CmdStan
code: data(algebra) fit <- rasch_fit(algebra, seed = 123) summary(fit) plot(fit, type = "wright")

title: Bayesian Rasch Models via CmdStan

what: Item Fit Statistics (Outfit and Infit) — item_fit
title: Item Fit Statistics (Outfit and Infit) — item_fit
text: Outfit = mean standardized squared residuals (sensitive outliers). Infit  = information-weighted mean-square (sensitive systematic misfit).

title: Item Fit Statistics (Outfit and Infit) — item_fit
code: item_fit(object)

title: Item Fit Statistics (Outfit and Infit) — item_fit
text: object fitted model rasch_fit(), twopl_fit(), threepl_fit().

title: Item Fit Statistics (Outfit and Infit) — item_fit
text: data frame columns: item, obs_prop, exp_prop, outfit, infit.

title: Item Fit Statistics (Outfit and Infit) — item_fit
text: near 1.0: Values greater 1.3 means noise model expects Values less 0.7 means less noise expected (items predictable)

title: Extract Item Difficulty Estimates (beta) — item_params
text: Returns tidy table estimated item difficulties credible intervals convergence diagnostics.

title: Extract Item Difficulty Estimates (beta) — item_params
code: item_params(object, prob = 0.95)

title: Extract Item Difficulty Estimates (beta) — item_params
text: object fitted model rasch_fit(). prob Width credible interval (0 1). Default 0.95 gives 95% credible interval.

title: Extract Item Difficulty Estimates (beta) — item_params
text: data frame columns: item Question name. mean Posterior mean — "best guess" point estimate. median Posterior median — robust alternative mean. sd Posterior SD — uncertain estimate. q_lower, q_upper Credible interval bounds. rhat Convergence diagnostic. Must < 1.01. ess_bulk Effective sample size (center). > 400. ess_tail Effective sample size (tails). > 400.

title: Plot a Rasch Model Fit — plot.birt_fit
text: x birt_fit object. type plot make: "icc" Item Characteristic Curves — P(correct) vs ability. "wright" Wright Map — persons & items scale. "info" Test Information Function — test measures best. "trace" MCMC trace plots — check chains mixed well. items items plot (integer indices). Default: . ... Extra arguments passed plotting function.

what: Fit a Bayesian Rasch (1PL) IRT Model — rasch_fit
title: Fit a Bayesian Rasch (1PL) IRT Model — rasch_fit
text: Fits Rasch model using CmdStan. model : logit(P(correct)) = alpha_j + delta - beta_k

title: Fit a Bayesian Rasch (1PL) IRT Model — rasch_fit
code: rasch_fit(   data,   prior_delta = c(0.75, 1),   prior_alpha_sd = 1,   prior_beta_sd = 1,   chains = 4,   parallel_chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000,   seed = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit a Bayesian Rasch (1PL) IRT Model — rasch_fit
text: data matrix data frame binary (0/1) responses. Rows = students, Columns = questions. NA allowed. prior_delta Prior mean ability: c(mean, sd). Default c(0.75, 1) means Normal(0.75, 1). prior_alpha_sd Prior SD student ability deviations. Default 1 means alpha ~ Normal(0, 1). prior_beta_sd Prior SD item difficulties. Default 1 means beta ~ Normal(0, 1). chains Number MCMC chains. Default 4. parallel_chains Chains run parallel. Default 4. iter_warmup Warmup iterations per chain. Default 1000. iter_sampling Sampling iterations per chain. Default 1000. seed Random seed reproducibility. ... Extra arguments passed cmdstanr's sample() method (e.g., adapt_delta = 0.95).

title: Fit a Bayesian Rasch (1PL) IRT Model — rasch_fit
text: object class birt_fit.

title: Fit a Bayesian Rasch (1PL) IRT Model — rasch_fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ sim <- rasch_simulate(J = 200, K = 10, seed = 42)  # Default priors fit <- rasch_fit(sim$data, seed = 123)  # Custom priors fit <- rasch_fit(sim$data,   prior_delta = c(0, 2),   prior_alpha_sd = 2,   prior_beta_sd = 2,   seed = 123 ) } # }

what: Simulate Data from a Rasch Model — rasch_simulate
title: Simulate Data from a Rasch Model — rasch_simulate
text: Creates fake response data KNOW true parameters. useful testing model works (parameter recovery), learning package, running simulation studies.

title: Simulate Data from a Rasch Model — rasch_simulate
code: rasch_simulate(   J = 200,   K = 10,   delta_true = 0.75,   alpha_sd = 1,   beta = NULL,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate Data from a Rasch Model — rasch_simulate
text: J Number students. Default 200. K Number questions. Default 10. delta_true True mean ability. Default 0.75 (matches prior). alpha_sd Standard deviation student ability deviations. Default 1. beta Values item difficulties. NULL, creates K evenly spaced values -2 (easiest) 2 (hardest). seed Random seed reproducibility. seed = data every time.

title: Simulate Data from a Rasch Model — rasch_simulate
text: list : data J x K matrix 0/1 responses. delta true mean ability used. alpha Vector true student deviations (length J). beta Vector true item difficulties (length K).

title: Simulate Data from a Rasch Model — rasch_simulate
code: sim <- rasch_simulate(J = 100, K = 5, seed = 42) head(sim$data) # first few rows of the response matrix #>    Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4 Q5 #> S1  1  1  1  1  1 #> S2  1  1  1  0  0 #> S3  1  1  1  1  1 #> S4  1  1  1  0  0 #> S5  1  1  0  1  0 #> S6  1  0  1  1  0 sim$beta # true item difficulties #> [1] -2 -1  0  1  2 sim$delta # true mean ability #> [1] 0.75

what: Summarize a Rasch Model Fit — summary.birt_fit
title: Summarize a Rasch Model Fit — summary.birt_fit
text: Displays item difficulties, person ability distribution, mean ability (delta), convergence diagnostics.

title: Summarize a Rasch Model Fit — summary.birt_fit
code: # S3 method for class 'birt_fit' summary(object, prob = 0.95, ...)

title: Summarize a Rasch Model Fit — summary.birt_fit
text: object birt_fit object. prob Credible interval width. Default 0.95. ... Ignored.

title: Fit a 3PL IRT Model — threepl_fit
text: Fits three-parameter logistic IRT model using CmdStan. model : P(correct) = c_k + (1 - c_k) * logistic(a_k * (alpha_j + delta - beta_k))

title: Fit a 3PL IRT Model — threepl_fit
code: threepl_fit(   data,   prior_delta = c(0.75, 1),   prior_alpha_sd = 1,   prior_beta_sd = 1,   prior_a = c(0, 0.5),   prior_c = c(5, 23),   chains = 4,   parallel_chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000,   seed = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit a 3PL IRT Model — threepl_fit
text: data matrix data frame binary (0/1) responses. Rows = students, Columns = questions. NA allowed. prior_delta Prior mean ability: c(mean, sd). Default c(0.75, 1). prior_alpha_sd Prior SD student ability deviations. Default 1. prior_beta_sd Prior SD item difficulties. Default 1. prior_a Prior discrimination (lognormal): c(meanlog, sdlog). Default c(0, 0.5). prior_c Prior guessing (beta distribution): c(alpha, beta). Default c(5, 23) means Beta(5, 23) mean 0.18. 4-option MC items, try c(5, 15) (mean 0.25). 5-option MC items, try c(5, 20) (mean 0.20). free-response items, try c(1, 19) (mean 0.05). chains Number MCMC chains. Default 4. parallel_chains Chains run parallel. Default 4. iter_warmup Warmup iterations per chain. Default 1000. iter_sampling Sampling iterations per chain. Default 1000. seed Random seed reproducibility. ... Extra arguments passed cmdstanr's sample() method.

title: Fit a 3PL IRT Model — threepl_fit
text: object class birt_3pl_fit.

title: Fit a 3PL IRT Model — threepl_fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ sim <- rasch_simulate(J = 500, K = 10, seed = 42)  # Default priors fit3 <- threepl_fit(sim$data, seed = 123)  # 4-option multiple choice fit3 <- threepl_fit(sim$data,   prior_c = c(5, 15),   seed = 123 ) } # }

title: Fit a 2PL IRT Model — twopl_fit
text: Fits two-parameter logistic IRT model using CmdStan. model : logit(P(correct)) = a_k * (alpha_j + delta - beta_k)

title: Fit a 2PL IRT Model — twopl_fit
code: twopl_fit(   data,   prior_delta = c(0.75, 1),   prior_alpha_sd = 1,   prior_beta_sd = 1,   prior_a = c(0, 0.5),   chains = 4,   parallel_chains = 4,   iter_warmup = 1000,   iter_sampling = 1000,   seed = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit a 2PL IRT Model — twopl_fit
text: data matrix data frame binary (0/1) responses. Rows = students, Columns = questions. NA allowed. prior_delta Prior mean ability: c(mean, sd). Default c(0.75, 1). prior_alpha_sd Prior SD student ability deviations. Default 1. prior_beta_sd Prior SD item difficulties. Default 1. prior_a Prior discrimination (lognormal): c(meanlog, sdlog). Default c(0, 0.5) means LogNormal(0, 0.5), centered around 1.0. chains Number MCMC chains. Default 4. parallel_chains Chains run parallel. Default 4. iter_warmup Warmup iterations per chain. Default 1000. iter_sampling Sampling iterations per chain. Default 1000. seed Random seed reproducibility. ... Extra arguments passed cmdstanr's sample() method.

title: Fit a 2PL IRT Model — twopl_fit
text: object class birt_2pl_fit.

title: Fit a 2PL IRT Model — twopl_fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ sim <- rasch_simulate(J = 300, K = 10, seed = 42)  # Default priors fit2 <- twopl_fit(sim$data, seed = 123)  # Custom priors fit2 <- twopl_fit(sim$data,   prior_a = c(0, 1),   seed = 123 ) } # }
